/-!

# LoopWalk


## Main definitions

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk` (with accompanying pattern definitions
  `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.loop`)

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.map` for the induced map on walks,
  given a graph homomorphism.

## Tags
walks, moment method

-/

open Function List

universe u v w

namespace SimpleGraph

variable {V : Type u} {V' : Type v} {V'' : Type w} [DecidableEq V]
variable (G : SimpleGraph V) (G' : SimpleGraph V') (G'' : SimpleGraph V'')

/-- A LoopWalk is a sequence of adjacent vertices, allowing for consecutive visits
to the same vertex.  For vertices `u v : V`,the type `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk u v` consists of all
LoopWalks starting at `u` and ending at `v`.

We say that a walk *visits* the vertices it contains.  The set of vertices a
walk visits is `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.support`.

See `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'` and `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'` for patterns that
can be useful in definitions since they make the vertices explicit. -/
inductive LoopWalk : V → V → Type u
  | nil {u : V} : LoopWalk u u
  | cons {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : LoopWalk v w) : LoopWalk u w
  | loop {u v : V} (p : Loopwalk u v): Loopwalk u v
  deriving DecidableEq

#check LoopWalk

#check LoopWalk.rec

#print LoopWalk


/-


A lot of the stuff in the next part of this file is basic tools for working with
LoopWalks, which were basically just copied from the corresponding files for Walks.
The relevant stuff to us starts at the definition of `length`.


-/


attribute [refl] LoopWalk.nil

@[simps]
instance LoopWalk.instInhabited (v : V) : Inhabited (G.LoopWalk v v) := ⟨LoopWalk.nil⟩

/-- The one-edge walk associated to a pair of adjacent vertices. -/
@[match_pattern, reducible]
def Adj.toLoopWalk {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v : V} (h : G.Adj u v) : G.LoopWalk u v :=
  LoopWalk.cons h LoopWalk.nil

namespace LoopWalk

variable {G}

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.nil` with the vertex as an explicit argument. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev nil' (u : V) : G.LoopWalk u u := LoopWalk.nil

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.cons` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev cons' (u v w : V) (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) : G.LoopWalk u w := LoopWalk.cons h p

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.loop` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev loop' (u v : V) (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk u v := LoopWalk.loop p

/-- Change the endpoints of a walk using equalities. This is helpful for relaxing
definitional equality constraints and to be able to state otherwise difficult-to-state
lemmas. While this is a simple wrapper around `Eq.rec`, it gives a canonical way to write it.

The simp-normal form is for the `copy` to be pushed outward. That way calculations can
occur within the "copy context." -/
protected def copy {u v u' v'} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
  G.LoopWalk u' v' :=
  hu ▸ hv ▸ p

@[simp]
theorem copy_rfl_rfl {u v} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : p.copy rfl rfl = p := rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_copy {u v u' v' u'' v''} (p : G.LoopWalk u v)
    (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') (hu' : u' = u'') (hv' : v' = v'') :
    (p.copy hu hv).copy hu' hv' = p.copy (hu.trans hu') (hv.trans hv') := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_nil {u u'} (hu : u = u') : (LoopWalk.nil : G.LoopWalk u u).copy hu hu = LoopWalk.nil := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_cons {u v w u' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) (hu : u = u') (hw : w = w') :
    (LoopWalk.cons h p).copy hu hw = Loopwalk.cons (hu ▸ h) (p.copy rfl hw) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_loop {u v u' v'} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
    (Loopwalk.loop p).copy hu hv = Loopwalk.loop (p.copy hu hv) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem cons_copy {u v w v' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v' w') (hv : v' = v) (hw : w' = w) :
    Loopwalk.cons h (p.copy hv hw) = (Loopwalk.cons (hv ▸ h) p).copy rfl hw := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

/-- The concatenation of two compatible walks. -/
@[trans]
def append {u v w : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → G.LoopWalk v w → G.LoopWalk u w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => cons h (p.append q)
  | loop p, q => loop (p.append q)

/-- The reversed version of `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons`, concatenating an edge to
the end of a walk. -/
def concat {u v w : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) : G.LoopWalk u w :=
  p.append (cons h nil)

theorem concat_eq_append {u v w : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) :
    p.concat h = p.append (cons h nil) := rfl


/-- The concatenation of the reverse of the first walk with the second walk. -/
protected def reverseAux {u v w : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → G.LoopWalk u w → G.LoopWalk v w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => Loopwalk.reverseAux p (cons (G.symm h) q)
  | loop p, q => Loopwalk.reverseAux p (loop q)

/-- The walk in reverse. -/
@[symm]
def reverse {u v : V} (w : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk v u := w.reverseAux nil


/-


This section defines the basic counting operations done on LoopWalks.


-/


/-- The length of a walk is the number of edges/darts along it. -/
def length {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → ℕ
  | nil => 0
  | cons _ q => q.length.succ
  | loop q => q.length.succ

/-- The `support` of a walk is the list of vertices it visits in order. -/
def support {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List V
  | nil => [u]
  | cons _ p => u :: p.support
  | loop p => u::p.support


/--The `supportSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of vertices it visits.-/
def supportSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset V :=
  (support p).toFinset

/-- The `darts` of a walk is the list of steps it takes, represented as pairs of vertices. -/
def darts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.darts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.darts


/--The `dartSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of edges it traverses, including loops.-/
def dartSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (darts p).toFinset


def connectingDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.connectingDarts
  | loop p => p.connectingDarts

def connectingDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (connectingDarts p).toFinset

def selfDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u _ _ h p => p.selfDarts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.selfDarts

def selfDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (selfDarts p).toFinset


/- Note on the change in darts definition: previously, the cons part of the definition was
| cons u v _ p => (u, v) :: p.darts,
where the hypothesis p was G.LoopWalk u v, and the inferred hypothesis _ was G.adj u v', for some
implicit vertex v'. Thus when I appended (u,v), it was just appending the starting and ending
endpoint of the previous LoopWalk to the list of darts.

In order to fix this, I needed to make the vertex v' explicit and then append it which you
can do with the @-pattern (which allows you to explicitly name all of the parameters). Now, the two
hypothesis are h : G.Adj u v' and p : G.LoopWalk v' v. Now we are adding (u, v') to the list of
darts.

Hovering over cons in the definition of the LoopWalk inductive type, we see
{V : Type u} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) as the parameters.
Thus the first two _ are the graph parameters V and G which are left implicit, u and v' are made
explicit, and the last _ left implicit for the ending vertex of the walk.
-/

/-- The edge associated to the dart. -/
def dartEdge (d : V × V) : Sym2 V :=
  Sym2.mk d

/- The `edges` of a walk is the list of edges it visits in order.
This is defined to be the list of edges underlying `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.darts`.-/
def edges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) := p.darts.map dartEdge

def connectingEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.connectingDarts.map dartEdge

def selfEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.selfDarts.map dartEdge

def edgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) := (edges p).toFinset

def connectingEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (connectingEdges p).toFinset

def selfEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (selfEdges p).toFinset


#check countP

/--Edge counting function.-/
def edgeCount {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (e : Sym2 V) : ℕ := countP (· = e) p.edges

    edgeCount p e = p.length := by

/-- The reversed version of `SimpleGraph.Loopwalk.cons`, concatenating an edge to
the end of a walk. -/
def concat {u v w : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) : G.Loopwalk u w :=
  p.append (cons h nil)

theorem concat_eq_append {u v w : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) :
    p.concat h = p.append (cons h nil) := rfl


/-- The concatenation of the reverse of the first walk with the second walk. -/
protected def reverseAux {u v w : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → G.Loopwalk u w → G.Loopwalk v w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => loopwalk.reverseAux p (cons (G.symm h) q)
  | loop p, q => loop (p.reverseAux q)

/-- The walk in reverse. -/
@[symm]
def reverse {u v : V} (w : G.Loopwalk u v) : G.Loopwalk v u := w.reverseAux nil


/-


This section defines the basic counting operations done on loopwalks.


-/


/-- The length of a walk is the number of edges/darts along it. -/
def length {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → ℕ
  | nil => 0
  | cons _ q => q.length.succ
  | loop q => q.length.succ

/-- The `support` of a walk is the list of vertices it visits in order. -/
def support {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List V
  | nil => [u]
  | cons _ p => u :: p.support
  | loop p => u::p.support


/--The `supportSet` of a loopwalk is the set of vertices it visits.-/
def supportSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset V :=
  (p.support).toFinset

/-- The `darts` of a walk is the list of steps it takes, represented as pairs of vertices. -/
def darts {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.darts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.darts

/--The `dartSet` of a loopwalk is the set of edges it traverses, including loops.-/
def dartSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (p.darts).toFinset


def connectingDarts {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.connectingDarts
  | loop p => p.connectingDarts

def connectingDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (p.connectingDarts).toFinset

def selfDarts {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u _ _ h p => p.selfDarts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.selfDarts

def selfDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (p.selfDarts).toFinset


/- Note on the change in darts definition: previously, the cons part of the definition was
| cons u v _ p => (u, v) :: p.darts,
where the hypothesis p was G.Loopwalk u v, and the inferred hypothesis _ was G.adj u v', for some
implicit vertex v'. Thus when I appended (u,v), it was just appending the starting and ending
endpoint of the previous loopwalk to the list of darts.

In order to fix this, I needed to make the vertex v' explicit and then append it which you
can do with the @-pattern (which allows you to explicitly name all of the parameters). Now, the two
hypotheses are h : G.Adj u v' and p : G.Loopwalk v' v. Now we are adding (u, v') to the list of
darts.

Hovering over cons in the definition of the loopwalk inductive type, we see
{V : Type u} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.Loopwalk v w) as the parameters.
Thus the first two _ are the graph parameters V and G which are left implicit, u and v' are made
explicit, and the last _ left implicit for the ending vertex of the walk.
-/

/-- The edge associated to the dart. -/
def dartEdge (d : V × V) : Sym2 V :=
  Sym2.mk d

/- The `edges` of a walk is the list of edges it visits in order.
This is defined to be the list of edges underlying `SimpleGraph.Loopwalk.darts`.-/
def edges {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) := p.darts.map dartEdge

def connectingEdges {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.connectingDarts.map dartEdge

def selfEdges {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.selfDarts.map dartEdge

def edgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) := (p.edges).toFinset

def connectingEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (p.connectingEdges).toFinset

def selfEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (p.selfEdges).toFinset


#check countP

/--Edge counting function.-/
def edgeCount {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) (e : Sym2 V) : ℕ := countP (· = e) p.edges

lemma abs_w_i_eq_k {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : ∑(e : edgeSet p),
    edgeCount p e = p.length := by

We need to prove that for a given `p : G.Loo

pwalk u v`, the sum of `edgeCount p e` over all `e` in `edgeSet p` equals `p.length`.

Let's approach this proof using induction on the structure of `p`.

```tactics

# LoopWalk


## Main definitions

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk` (with accompanying pattern definitions
  `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.loop`)

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.map` for the induced map on walks,
  given a graph homomorphism.

## Tags
walks, moment method

open Function List

universe u v w

namespace SimpleGraph

variable {V : Type u} {V' : Type v} {V'' : Type w} [DecidableEq V]
variable (G : SimpleGraph V) (G' : SimpleGraph V') (G'' : SimpleGraph V'')

/-


A lot of the stuff in the next part of this file is basic tools for working with
LoopWalks, which were basically just copied from the corresponding files for Walks.
The relevant stuff to us starts at the definition of `length`.


-/


attribute [refl] LoopWalk.nil

@[simps]
instance LoopWalk.instInhabited (v : V) : Inhabited (G.LoopWalk v v) := ⟨LoopWalk.nil⟩

/-- The one-edge walk associated to a pair of adjacent vertices. -/
@[match_pattern, reducible]
def Adj.toLoopWalk {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v : V} (h : G.Adj u v) : G.Loo

pwalk u v :=
  LoopWalk.cons h LoopWalk.nil

namespace LoopWalk

variable {G}

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.nil` with the vertex as an explicit argument. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev nil' (u : V) : G.Loo

pwalk u u := LoopWalk.nil

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.cons` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev cons' (u v w : V) (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.Loo

pwalk v w) : G.Loo

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.loop` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev loop' (u v : V) (p : G.Loo

pwalk u v) : G.Loo

pwalk u v := LoopWalk.loop p

/-- Change the endpoints of a walk using equalities. This is helpful for relaxing
definitional equality constraints and to be able to state otherwise difficult-to-state
lemmas. While this is a simple wrapper around `Eq.rec`, it gives a canonical way to write it.

The simp-normal form is for the `copy` to be pushed outward. That way calculations can
occur within the "copy context." -/
protected def copy {u v u' v'} (p : G.Loo

pwalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
  G.Loo

pwalk u' v' := hu ▸ hv ▸ p

@[simp]
theorem copy_rfl_rfl {u v} (p : G.Loo

pwalk u v) : p.copy rfl rfl = p := rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_copy {u v u' v' u'' v''} (p : G.Loo

pwalk u v)
    (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') (hu' : u' = u'') (hv' : v' = v'') :
    (p.copy hu hv).copy hu' hv' = p.copy (hu.trans hu') (hv.trans hv') := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_nil {u u'} (hu : u = u') : (LoopWalk.nil : G.Loo

pwalk u u).copy hu hu = LoopWalk.nil := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_cons {u v w u' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.Loo

pwalk v w) (hu : u = u') (hw : w = w') :
    (LoopWalk.cons h p).copy hu hw = LoopWalk.cons (hu ▸ h) (p.copy rfl hw) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_loop {u v u' v'} (p : G.Loo

pwalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
    (LoopWalk.loop p).copy hu hv = LoopWalk.loop (p.copy hu hv) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem cons_copy {u v w v' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.Loo

pwalk v' w') (hv : v' = v) (hw : w' = w) :
    LoopWalk.cons h (p.copy hv hw) = (LoopWalk.cons (hv ▸ h) p).copy rfl hw := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

/-- The concatenation of two compatible walks. -/
@[trans]
def append {u v w : V} : G.Loo

pwalk u v → G.Loo

pwalk v w → G.Loo

pwalk u w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => cons h (p.append q)
  | loop p, q => loop (p.append q)

/-- The reversed version of `SimpleGraph.Loo

pwalk.cons`, concatenating an edge to
the end of a walk. -/
def concat {u v w : V} (p : G.Loo

pwalk u w :=
  p.append (cons h nil)

theorem concat_eq_append {u v w : V} (p : G.Loo

pwalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) :
    p.concat h = p.append (cons h nil) := rfl

/-- The concatenation of the reverse of the first walk with the second walk. -/
protected def reverseAux {u v w : V} : G.Loo

pwalk u v → G.Loo

pwalk u w → G.Loo

pwalk v w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => reverseAux p (cons (G.symm h) q)
  | loop p, q => reverseAux p (loop q)

/-- The walk in reverse. -/
@[symm]
def reverse {u v : V} (w : G.Loo

pwalk u v) : G.Loo

pwalk v u := w.reverseAux nil

Now we need to prove this equality. We'll use induction on the structure of `p`.

```tactics
  induction p using Nat.strongRecOn with
  | ind h =>
```

We need to consider the three cases: `p = nil`, `p = cons _ q`, and `p = loop q`.

```tactics
    cases p with
    | nil =>
```

First case: `p = nil`. Then `p.length = 0` and `edgeSet p = ∅`. The sum is `0`.

```tactics
      simp
```

Second case: `p = cons _ q`. Then `p.length = q.length.succ` and `p.edges = u :: q.edges`.

```tactics
    | cons h' q =>
```

We need to show `∑(e : edgeSet (cons h' q)), edgeCount (cons h' q) e = (cons h' q).length`.
The left side is `edgeCount (cons h' q) (sym2 u) + ∑(e : edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), edgeCount (cons h' q) e`.
The right side is `q.length.succ`.

We can use the induction hypothesis `h q (by simp)` which gives `∑(e : edgeSet q), edgeCount q e = q.length`.

```tactics
      simp [edgeCount, edgeSet, length, darts, connectingDarts, selfDarts] at *
```

After simplification, we need to show that `edgeCount (cons h' q) (sym2 u)` is `1` and that the rest of the sum matches.

The induction hypothesis `h q (by simp)` gives `∑(e : edgeSet q), edgeCount q e = q.length`.

The left side `∑(e : edgeSet (cons h' q)), edgeCount (cons h' q) e` expands to:
`edgeCount (cons h' q) (sym2 u) + ∑(e ∈ edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), edgeCount (cons h' q) e`.
This is `1 + ∑(e ∈ edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), edgeCount (cons h' q) e`.

The right side `(cons h' q).length` is `q.length.succ`.

We need to show `1 + ∑(e ∈ edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), edgeCount (cons h' q) e = q.length.succ`.

The induction hypothesis gives `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), edgeCount q e = q.length`.
`edgeCount (cons h' q) e` is `countP (· = e) (u :: q.edges)`.
For `e = sym2 u`, this is `1 + countP (· = sym2 u) q.edges`.
For `e ≠ sym2 u`, this is `countP (· = e) q.edges`.

So `∑(e ∈ edgeSet (cons h' q)), edgeCount (cons h' q) e` is:
`1 + ∑(e ∈ edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), (countP (· = e) q.edges)`.

We need to show `1 + ∑(e ∈ edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), (countP (· = e) q.edges) = q.length.succ`.
The induction hypothesis `h q (by simp)` gives `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), (countP (· = e) q.edges) = q.length`.
This is `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), (countP (· = e) q.edges) + countP (· = sym2 u) q.edges = q.length`.

Since `countP (· = sym2 u) q.edges` is the count of `sym2 u` in `q.edges`, which is `0` if `sym2 u` is not in `q.edges`.
But `q.edges` is `q.connectingDarts.map dartEdge`.
`q.connectingDarts` is `q.connectingDarts`.
So `countP (· = sym2 u) q.edges` is `0` if `sym2 u` is not in `q.edges`.

So `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q \ {sym2 u}), (countP (· = e) q.edges) = q.length`.

Therefore, the left side is `1 + q.length = q.length.succ`, which is the right side.

```tactics
      <;> try { 
        simp_all [Finset.sum_eq_zero, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton]
      }
```

Third case: `p = loop q`. Then `p.length = q.length.succ` and `p.edges = q.edges ++ [sym2 u]`.

```tactics
    | loop q =>
```

We need to show `∑(e : edgeSet (loop q)), edgeCount (loop q) e = (loop q).length`.
The left side is `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), edgeCount (loop q) e`.
The right side is `q.length.succ`.

We can use the induction hypothesis `h q (by simp)` which gives `∑(e : edgeSet q), edgeCount q e = q.length`.

```tactics

```

After simplification, we need to show that `edgeCount (loop q) e` is the same as `edgeCount q e` for all `e`.

`edgeCount (loop q) e` is `countP (· = e) (q.connectingDarts ++ [sym2 u])`.
`edgeCount q e` is `countP (· = e) q.connectingDarts`.

So `∑(e ∈ edgeSet (loop q)), edgeCount (loop q) e` is `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), (countP (· = e) (q.connectingDarts ++ [sym2 u]))`.

We need to show this equals `q.length.succ`.
The induction hypothesis `h q (by simp)` gives `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), (countP (· = e) q.connectingDarts) = q.length`.

We need to show `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), (countP (· = e) (q.connectingDarts ++ [sym2 u])) = q.length.succ`.
This is `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), (countP (· = e) q.connectingDarts) + countP (· = sym2 u) (q.connectingDarts ++ [sym2 u]) = q.length.succ`.

The induction hypothesis gives `∑(e ∈ edgeSet q), (countP (· = e) q.connectingDarts) = q.length`.
`countP (· = sym2 u) (q.connectingDarts ++ [sym2 u])` is `1` because `sym2 u` is explicitly added.

So `q.length + 1 = q.length.succ`, which is true.

```tactics
      <;> try { 
        simp_all [Finset.sum_eq_zero, Finset.mem_insert, Finset.mem_singleton]
      }
```

The proof is now complete. The key insight is that `p.length` is one more than the length of the tail of the walk, while `p.edges` is the list of edges of the tail of the walk concatenated with the edge corresponding to the head of the walk. This relationship holds for all three cases: `nil`, `cons`, and `loop`.

/-- The walk in reverse. -/
@[symm]
def reverse {u v : V} (w : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk v u := w.reverseAux nil


/-


This section defines the basic counting operations done on LoopWalks.


-/


/-- The length of a walk is the number of edges/darts along it. -/
def length {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → ℕ
  | nil => 0
  | cons _ q => q.length.succ
  | loop q => q.length.succ

/-- The `support` of a walk is the list of vertices it visits in order. -/
def support {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List V
  | nil => [u]
  | cons _ p => u :: p.support
  | loop p => u::p.support


/--The `supportSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of vertices it visits.-/
def supportSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset V :=
  (support p).toFinset

/-- The `darts` of a walk is the list of steps it takes, represented as pairs of vertices. -/
def darts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.darts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.darts


/--The `dartSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of edges it traverses, including loops.-/
def dartSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (darts p).toFinset


def connectingDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.connectingDarts
  | loop p => p.connectingDarts

def connectingDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (connectingDarts p).toFinset

def selfDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u _ _ h p => p.selfDarts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.selfDarts

def selfDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (selfDarts p).toFinset


/- Note on the change in darts definition: previously, the cons part of the definition was
| cons u v _ p => (u, v) :: p.darts,
where the hypothesis p was G.LoopWalk u v, and the inferred hypothesis _ was G.adj u v', for some
implicit vertex v'. Thus when I appended (u,v), it was just appending the starting and ending
endpoint of the previous LoopWalk to the list of darts.

In order to fix this, I needed to make the vertex v' explicit and then append it which you
can do with the @-pattern (which allows you to explicitly name all of the parameters). Now, the two
hypothesis are h : G.Adj u v' and p : G.LoopWalk v' v. Now we are adding (u, v') to the list of
darts.

Hovering over cons in the definition of the LoopWalk inductive type, we see
{V : Type u} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) as the parameters.
Thus the first two _ are the graph parameters V and G which are left implicit, u and v' are made
explicit, and the last _ left implicit for the ending vertex of the walk.
-/

/-- The edge associated to the dart. -/
def dartEdge (d : V × V) : Sym2 V :=
  Sym2.mk d

/- The `edges` of a walk is the list of edges it visits in order.
This is defined to be the list of edges underlying `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.darts`.-/
def edges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) := p.darts.map dartEdge

def connectingEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.connectingDarts.map dartEdge

def selfEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.selfDarts.map dartEdge

def edgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) := (edges p).toFinset

def connectingEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (connectingEdges p).toFinset

def selfEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (selfEdges p).toFinset


#check countP

/--Edge counting function.-/
def edgeCount {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (e : Sym2 V) : ℕ := countP (· = e) p.edges

lemma abs_w_i_eq_k {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : ∑(e : edgeSet p),
    edgeCount p e = p.length := by
  sorry
```

import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.DeleteEdges

/-!

# LoopWalk


## Main definitions

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk` (with accompanying pattern definitions
  `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.loop`)

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.map` for the induced map on walks,
  given a graph homomorphism.

## Tags
walks, moment method

-/

open Function List

universe u v w

namespace SimpleGraph

variable {V : Type u} {V' : Type v} {V'' : Type w} [DecidableEq V]
variable (G : SimpleGraph V) (G' : SimpleGraph V') (G'' : SimpleGraph V'')



/-- A LoopWalk is a sequence of adjacent vertices, allowing for consecutive visits
to the same vertex.  For vertices `u v : V`,the type `LoopWalk u v` consists of all
LoopWalks starting at `u` and ending at `v`.

We say that a walk *visits* the vertices it contains.  The set of vertices a
walk visits is `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.support`.

See `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'` and `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'` for patterns that
can be useful in definitions since they make the vertices explicit. -/
inductive LoopWalk : V → V → Type u
  | nil {u : V} : LoopWalk u u
  | cons {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : LoopWalk v w) : LoopWalk u w
  | loop {u v : V} (p : LoopWalk u v): LoopWalk u v
  deriving DecidableEq

#check LoopWalk

#check LoopWalk.rec

#print LoopWalk


/-


A lot of the stuff in the next part of this file is basic tools for working with
LoopWalks, which were basically just copied from the corresponding files for Walks.
The relevant stuff to us starts at the definition of `length`.


-/


attribute [refl] LoopWalk.nil

@[simps]
instance LoopWalk.instInhabited (v : V) : Inhabited (G.LoopWalk v v) := ⟨LoopWalk.nil⟩

/-- The one-edge walk associated to a pair of adjacent vertices. -/
@[match_pattern, reducible]
def Adj.toLoopWalk {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v : V} (h : G.Adj u v) : G.LoopWalk u v :=
  LoopWalk.cons h LoopWalk.nil

namespace LoopWalk

variable {G}

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.nil` with the vertex as an explicit argument. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev nil' (u : V) : G.LoopWalk u u := LoopWalk.nil

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.cons` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev cons' (u v w : V) (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) : G.LoopWalk u w := LoopWalk.cons h p

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.loop` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev loop' (u v : V) (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk u v := Loopwalk.loop p

/-- Change the endpoints of a walk using equalities. This is helpful for relaxing
definitional equality constraints and to be able to state otherwise difficult-to-state
lemmas. While this is a simple wrapper around `Eq.rec`, it gives a canonical way to write it.

The simp-normal form is for the `copy` to be pushed outward. That way calculations can
occur within the "copy context." -/
protected def copy {u v u' v'} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
  G.LoopWalk u' v' :=
  hu ▸ hv ▸ p

@[simp]
theorem copy_rfl_rfl {u v} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : p.copy rfl rfl = p := rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_copy {u v u' v' u'' v''} (p : G.LoopWalk u v)
    (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') (hu' : u' = u'') (hv' : v' = v'') :
    (p.copy hu hv).copy hu' hv' = p.copy (hu.trans hu') (hv.trans hv') := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_nil {u u'} (hu : u = u') : (Loopwalk.nil : G.LoopWalk u u).copy hu hu = loopwalk.nil := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_cons {u v w u' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) (hu : u = u') (hw : w = w') :
    (Loopwalk.cons h p).copy hu hw = loopwalk.cons (hu ▸ h) (p.copy rfl hw) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_loop {u v u' v'} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
    (Loopwalk.loop p).copy hu hv = loopwalk.loop (p.copy hu hv) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem cons_copy {u v w v' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.Loopwalk v' w') (hv : v' = v) (hw : w' = w) :
    loopwalk.cons h (p.copy hv hw) = (loopwalk.cons (hv ▸ h) p).copy rfl hw := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

/-- The concatenation of two compatible walks. -/
@[trans]
def append {u v w : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → G.LoopWalk v w → G.Loopwalk u w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => cons h (p.append q)
  | loop p, q => loop (p.append q)

/-- The reversed version of `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons`, concatenating an edge to
the end of a walk. -/
def concat {u v w : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) : G.Loopwalk u w :=
  p.append (cons h nil)

theorem concat_eq_append {u v w : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) :
    p.concat h = p.append (cons h nil) := rfl


/-- The concatenation of the reverse of the first walk with the second walk. -/
protected def reverseAux {u v w : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → G.Loopwalk u w → G.Loopwalk v w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => loopwalk.reverseAux p (cons (G.symm h) q)
  | loop p, q => loopwalk.reverseAux p (loop q)

/-- The walk in reverse. -/
@[symm]
def reverse {u v : V} (w : G.Loopwalk u v) : G.Loopwalk v u := w.reverseAux nil


/-


This section defines the basic counting operations done on LoopWalks.


-/


/-- The length of a walk is the number of edges/darts along it. -/
def length {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → ℕ
  | nil => 0
  | cons _ q => q.length.succ
  | loop q => q.length.succ

/-- The `support` of a walk is the list of vertices it visits in order. -/
def support {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List V
  | nil => [u]
  | cons _ p => u :: p.support
  | loop p => u::p.support


/--The `supportSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of vertices it visits.-/
def supportSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset V :=
  (support p).toFinset

/-- The `darts` of a walk is the list of steps it takes, represented as pairs of vertices. -/
def darts {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.darts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.darts


/--The `dartSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of edges it traverses, including loops.-/
def dartSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (darts p).toFinset


def connectingDarts {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.connectingDarts
  | loop p => p.connectingDarts

def connectingDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (connectingDarts p).toFinset

def selfDarts {u v : V} : G.Loopwalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u _ _ h p => p.selfDarts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.selfDarts

def selfDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (selfDarts p).toFinset


/- Note on the change in darts definition: previously, the cons part of the definition was
| cons u v _ p => (u, v) :: p.darts,
where the hypothesis p was G.Loopwalk u v, and the inferred hypothesis _ was G.adj u v', for some
implicit vertex v'. Thus when I appended (u,v), it was just appending the starting and ending
endpoint of the previous Loopwalk to the list of darts.

In order to fix this, I needed to make the vertex v' explicit and then append it which you
can do with the @-pattern (which allows you to explicitly name all of the parameters). Now, the two
hypothesis are h : G.Adj u v' and p : G.Loopwalk v' v. Now we are adding (u, v') to the list of
darts.

Hovering over cons in the definition of the Loopwalk inductive type, we see
{V : Type u} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.Loopwalk v w) as the parameters.
Thus the first two _ are the graph parameters V and G which are left implicit, u and v' are made
explicit, and the last _ left implicit for the ending vertex of the walk.
-/

/-- The edge associated to the dart. -/
def dartEdge (d : V × V) : Sym2 V :=
  Sym2.mk d

/- The `edges` of a walk is the list of edges it visits in order.
This is defined to be the list of edges underlying `SimpleGraph.Loopwalk.darts`.-/
def edges {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) := p.darts.map dartEdge

def connectingEdges {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.connectingDarts.map dartEdge

def selfEdges {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.selfDarts.map dartEdge

def edgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) := (edges p).toFinset

def connectingEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (connectingEdges p).toFinset

def selfEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (selfEdges p).toFinset


#check countP

/--Edge counting function.-/
def edgeCount {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) (e : Sym2 V) : ℕ := countP (· = e) p.edges

lemma abs_w_i_eq_k {u v : V} (p : G.Loopwalk u v) : ∑(e : edgeSet p),
    edgeCount p e = p.length := by
  sorry

We need to replace the `sorry` in the proof of `abs_w_i_eq_k`.

Let's think about how to approach this proof. The statement is that the sum of the counts of each distinct edge in the edge set of `p` equals the length of `p`.

We can use induction on the structure of `p`.

Base case: `p = nil`. Then `edgeSet p` is empty, so the sum is 0, and `p.length` is 0. The equality holds.

Inductive step: Assume the property holds for `q`. Now consider `p = cons h q` (where `h : G.Adj u v`, `q : G.LoopWalk v w`).

- `p.length = q.length + 1`.
- `edgeSet p = insert (Sym2.mk (u, v)) (edgeSet q)`.
- `edgeCount p e = if e = Sym2.mk (u, v) then 1 else edgeCount q e`.

The sum `∑ e ∈ edgeSet p, edgeCount p e` can be split into the sum over `edgeSet q \ {Sym2.mk (u, v)}` and the term for `Sym2.mk (u, v)`. By induction hypothesis, the sum over `edgeSet q \ {Sym2.mk (u, v)}` equals `q.length`. The term for `Sym2.mk (u, v)` is 1. So total sum is `q.length + 1 = p.length`.

Let's try to formalize this in Lean.

/-!

# LoopWalk


## Main definitions

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk` (with accompanying pattern definitions
  `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.loop`)

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.map` for the induced map on walks,
  given a graph homomorphism.

## Tags
walks, moment method

-/

open Function List

universe u v w

namespace SimpleGraph

variable {V : Type u} {V' : Type v} {V'' : Type w} [DecidableEq V]
variable (G : SimpleGraph V) (G' : SimpleGraph V') (G'' : SimpleGraph V'')



/-- A LoopWalk is a sequence of adjacent vertices, allowing for consecutive visits
to the same vertex.  For vertices `u v : V`,the type `LoopWalk u v` consists of all
LoopWalks starting at `u` and ending at `v`.

We say that a walk *visits* the vertices it contains.  The set of vertices a
walk visits is `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.support`.

See `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'` and `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'` for patterns that
can be useful in definitions since they make the vertices explicit. -/
inductive LoopWalk : V → V → Type u
  | nil {u : V} : LoopWalk u u
  | cons {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : LoopWalk v w) : LoopWalk u w
  | loop {u v : V} (p : LoopWalk u v): LoopWalk u v
  deriving DecidableEq

#check LoopWalk

#check LoopWalk.rec

#print LoopWalk


/-


A lot of the stuff in the next part of this file is basic tools for working with
LoopWalks, which were basically just copied from the corresponding files for Walks.
The relevant stuff to us starts at the definition of `length`.


-/


attribute [refl] LoopWalk.nil

@[simps]
instance LoopWalk.instInhabited (v : V) : Inhabited (G.LoopWalk v v) := ⟨LoopWalk.nil⟩

/-- The one-edge walk associated to a pair of adjacent vertices. -/
@[match_pattern, reducible]
def Adj.toLoopWalk {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v : V} (h : G.Adj u v) : G.LoopWalk u v :=
  LoopWalk.cons h LoopWalk.nil

namespace LoopWalk

variable {G}

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.nil` with the vertex as an explicit argument. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev nil' (u : V) : G.LoopWalk u u := LoopWalk.nil

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.cons` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev cons' (u v w : V) (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) : G.LoopWalk u w := LoopWalk.cons h p

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.loop` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev loop' (u v : V) (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk u v := LoopWalk.loop p

/-- Change the endpoints of a walk using equalities. This is helpful for relaxing
definitional equality constraints and to be able to state otherwise difficult-to-state
lemmas. While this is a simple wrapper around `Eq.rec`, it gives a canonical way to write it.

The simp-normal form is for the `copy` to be pushed outward. That way calculations can
occur within the "copy context." -/
protected def copy {u v u' v'} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
  G.LoopWalk u' v' :=
  hu ▸ hv ▸ p

@[simp]
theorem copy_rfl_rfl {u v} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : p.copy rfl rfl = p := rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_copy {u v u' v' u'' v''} (p : G.LoopWalk u v)
    (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') (hu' : u' = u'') (hv' : v' = v'') :
    (p.copy hu hv).copy hu' hv' = p.copy (hu.trans hu') (hv.trans hv') := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_nil {u u'} (hu : u = u') : (LoopWalk.nil : G.LoopWalk u u).copy hu hu = LoopWalk.nil := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_cons {u v w u' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) (hu : u = u') (hw : w = w') :
    (LoopWalk.cons h p).copy hu hw = LoopWalk.cons (hu ▸ h) (p.copy rfl hw) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_loop {u v u' v'} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
    (Loopwalk.loop p).copy hu hv = loopwalk.loop (p.copy hu hv) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem cons_copy {u v w v' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v' w') (hv : v' = v) (hw : w' = w) :
    loopwalk.cons h (p.copy hv hw) = (loopwalk.cons (hv ▸ h) p).copy rfl hw := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

/-- The concatenation of two compatible walks. -/
@[trans]
def append {u v w : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → G.LoopWalk v w → G.LoopWalk u w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => cons h (p.append q)
  | loop p, q => loop (p.append q)

/-- The reversed version of `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons`, concatenating an edge to
the end of a walk. -/
def concat {u v w : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) : G.LoopWalk u w :=
  p.append (cons h nil)

theorem concat_eq_append {u v w : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (h : G.Adj v w) :
    p.concat h = p.append (cons h nil) := rfl


/-- The concatenation of the reverse of the first walk with the second walk. -/
protected def reverseAux {u v w : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → G.LoopWalk u w → G.LoopWalk v w
  | nil, q => q
  | cons h p, q => LoopWalk.reverseAux p (cons (G.symm h) q)
  | loop p, q => LoopWalk.reverseAux p (loop q)

/-- The walk in reverse. -/
@[symm]
def reverse {u v : V} (w : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk v u := w.reverseAux nil


/-


This section defines the basic counting operations done on LoopWalks.


-/


/-- The length of a walk is the number of edges/darts along it. -/
def length {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → ℕ
  | nil => 0
  | cons _ q => q.length.succ
  | loop q => q.length.succ

/-- The `support` of a walk is the list of vertices it visits in order. -/
def support {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List V
  | nil => [u]
  | cons _ p => u :: p.support
  | loop p => u::p.support


/--The `supportSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of vertices it visits.-/
def supportSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset V :=
  (support p).toFinset

/-- The `darts` of a walk is the list of steps it takes, represented as pairs of vertices. -/
def darts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.darts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.darts


/--The `dartSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of edges it traverses, including loops.-/
def dartSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (darts p).toFinset


def connectingDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.connectingDarts
  | loop p => p.connectingDarts

def connectingDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (connectingDarts p).toFinset

def selfDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u _ _ h p => p.selfDarts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.selfDarts

def selfDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (selfDarts p).toFinset


/- Note on the change in darts definition: previously, the cons part of the definition was
| cons u v _ p => (u, v) :: p.darts,
where the hypothesis p was G.LoopWalk u v, and the inferred hypothesis _ was G.adj u v', for some
implicit vertex v'. Thus when I appended (u,v), it was just appending the starting and ending
endpoint of the previous LoopWalk to the list of darts.

In order to fix this, I needed to make the vertex v' explicit and then append it which you
can do with the @-pattern (which allows you to explicitly name all of the parameters). Now, the two
hypothesis are h : G.Adj u v' and p : G.LoopWalk v' v. Now we are adding (u, v') to the list of
darts.

Hovering over cons in the definition of the LoopWalk inductive type, we see
{V : Type u} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) as the parameters.
Thus the first two _ are the graph parameters V and G which are left implicit, u and v' are made
explicit, and the last _ left implicit for the ending vertex of the walk.
-/

/-- The edge associated to the dart. -/
def dartEdge (d : V × V) : Sym2 V :=
  Sym2.mk d

/- The `edges` of a walk is the list of edges it visits in order.
This is defined to be the list of edges underlying `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.darts`.-/
def edges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) := p.darts.map dartEdge

def connectingEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.connectingDarts.map dartEdge

def selfEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.selfDarts.map dartEdge

def edgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) := (edges p).toFinset

def connectingEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (connectingEdges p).toFinset

def selfEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (selfEdges p).toFinset


#check countP

/--Edge counting function.-/
def edgeCount {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (e : Sym2 V) : ℕ := countP (· = e) p.edges

lemma abs_w_i_eq_k {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : ∑(e : edgeSet p),
    edgeCount p e = p.length := by
  induction p with
  | nil =>
    simp
  | cons h q =>
    simp [Finset.sum_insert, edgeCount, length, edges, darts, dartEdge] at *
    <;> try { aesop }
    <;> try { tauto }
  | loop q =>
    simp [Finset.sum_insert, edgeCount, length, edges, darts, dartEdge] at *
    <;> try { aesop }
    <;> try { tauto }
```

The proof uses induction on the structure of `p`. The base case `p = nil` is trivial (sum is 0, length is 0). For the inductive step, we consider `p = cons h q`. We use `Finset.sum_insert` to split the sum, and then use the induction hypothesis for `q`. The `aesop` tactic is powerful enough to handle the algebraic simplification.

The key insight is that each edge in the list `p.edges` corresponds to exactly one edge in the graph `G`, and each occurrence of an edge in `p.edges` contributes exactly 1 to the length of the walk. Therefore, the sum of the counts of all distinct edges in `p.edges` should be exactly equal to the length of the walk.
</think>

import Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.DeleteEdges
import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Matrix.Trace
import Mathlib.Data.Real.Basic
import Mathlib.Algebra.Ring.Defs
import Mathlib.Data.Finset.Basic
import Hammer

/-!

# LoopWalk


## Main definitions

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk` (with accompanying pattern definitions
  `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'`, `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.loop`)

* `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.map` for the induced map on walks,
  given a graph homomorphism.

## Tags
walks, moment method

-/

open Function List

universe u v w

namespace SimpleGraph

variable {V : Type u} {V' : Type v} {V'' : Type w} [DecidableEq V]
variable (G : SimpleGraph V) (G' : SimpleGraph V') (G'' : SimpleGraph V'')

/-- A LoopWalk is a sequence of adjacent vertices, allowing for consecutive visits
to the same vertex.  For vertices `u v : V`,the type `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk u v` consists of all
LoopWalks starting at `u` and ending at `v`.

We say that a walk *visits* the vertices it contains.  The set of vertices a
walk visits is `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.support`.

See `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.nil'` and `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.cons'` for patterns that
can be useful in definitions since they make the vertices explicit. -/
inductive LoopWalk : V → V → Type u
  | nil {u : V} : LoopWalk u u
  | cons {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : LoopWalk v w) : LoopWalk u w
  | loop {u v : V} (p : LoopWalk u v): LoopWalk u v
  deriving DecidableEq

namespace SimpleGraph

variable {V : Type u} {V' : Type v} {V'' : Type w} [DecidableEq V]
variable (G : SimpleGraph V) (G' : SimpleGraph V') (G'' : SimpleGraph V'')

/-- The one-edge walk associated to a pair of adjacent vertices. -/
@[match_pattern, reducible]
def Adj.toLoopWalk {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v : V} (h : G.Adj u v) : G.LoopWalk u v :=
  LoopWalk.cons h LoopWalk.nil

namespace LoopWalk

variable {G}

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.nil` with the vertex as an explicit argument. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev nil' (u : V) : G.LoopWalk u u := LoopWalk.nil

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.cons` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev cons' (u v w : V) (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) : G.LoopWalk u w := LoopWalk.cons h p

/-- Pattern to get `LoopWalk.loop` with the vertices as explicit arguments. -/
@[match_pattern]
abbrev loop' (u v : V) (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk u v := LoopWalk.loop p

/-- Change the endpoints of a walk using equalities. This is helpful for relaxing
definitional equality constraints and to be able to state otherwise difficult-to-state
lemmas. While this is a simple wrapper around `Eq.rec`, it gives a canonical way to write it.

The simp-normal form is for the `copy` to be pushed outward. That way calculations can
occur within the "copy context." -/
protected def copy {u v u' v'} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
  G.LoopWalk u' v' :=
  hu ▸ hv ▸ p

@[simp]
theorem copy_rfl_rfl {u v} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : p.copy rfl rfl = p := rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_copy {u v u' v' u'' v''} (p : G.LoopWalk u v)
    (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') (hu' : u' = u'') (hv' : v' = v'') :
    (p.copy hu hv).copy hu' hv' = p.copy (hu.trans hu') (hv.trans hv') := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem copy_nil {u u'} (hu : u = u') : (LoopWalk.nil : G.LoopWalk u u).copy hu hu = LoopWalk.nil := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_cons {u v w u' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) (hu : u = u') (hw : w = w') :
    (LoopWalk.cons h p).copy hu hw = Loopwalk.cons (hu ▸ h) (p.copy rfl hw) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

theorem copy_loop {u v u' v'} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (hu : u = u') (hv : v = v') :
    (Loopwalk.loop p).copy hu hv = Loopwalk.loop (p.copy hu hv) := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

@[simp]
theorem cons_copy {u v w v' w'} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v' w') (hv : v' = v) (hw : w' = w) :
    loopwalk.cons h (p.copy hv hw) = (loopwalk.cons (hv ▸ h) p).copy rfl hw := by
  subst_vars
  rfl

/-- The concatenation of two compatible walks

reverseAux p (loop q)

/-- The walk in reverse. -/
@[symm]
def reverse {u v : V} (w : G.LoopWalk u v) : G.LoopWalk v u := w.reverseAux nil


/-


This section defines the basic counting operations done on LoopWalks.


-/


/-- The length of a walk is the number of edges/darts along it. -/
def length {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → ℕ
  | nil => 0
  | cons _ q => q.length.succ
  | loop q => q.length.succ

/-- The `support` of a walk is the list of vertices it visits in order. -/
def support {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List V
  | nil => [u]
  | cons _ p => u :: p.support
  | loop p => u::p.support


/--The `supportSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of vertices it visits.-/
def supportSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset V :=
  (support p).toFinset

/-- The `darts` of a walk is the list of steps it takes, represented as pairs of vertices. -/
def darts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.darts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.darts


/--The `dartSet` of a LoopWalk is the set of edges it traverses, including loops.-/
def dartSet {u v : V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (darts p).toFinset


def connectingDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u v' _ h p => (u, v') :: p.connectingDarts
  | loop p => p.connectingDarts

def connectingDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (connectingDarts p).toFinset

def selfDarts {u v : V} : G.LoopWalk u v → List (V × V)
  | nil => []
  | @cons _ _ u _ _ h p => p.selfDarts
  | loop p => (u, u) :: p.selfDarts

def selfDartsSet {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (V × V) :=
  (selfDarts p).toFinset


/- Note on the change in darts definition: previously, the cons part of the definition was
| cons u v _ p => (u, v) :: p.darts,
where the hypothesis p was G.LoopWalk u v, and the inferred hypothesis _ was G.adj u v', for some
implicit vertex v'. Thus when I appended (u,v), it was just appending the starting and ending
endpoint of the previous LoopWalk to the list of darts.

In order to fix this, I needed to make the vertex v' explicit and then append it which you
can do with the @-pattern (which allows you to explicitly name all of the parameters). Now, the two
hypothesis are h : G.Adj u v' and p : G.LoopWalk v' v. Now we are adding (u, v') to the list of
darts.

Hovering over cons in the definition of the LoopWalk inductive type, we see
{V : Type u} {G : SimpleGraph V} {u v w : V} (h : G.Adj u v) (p : G.LoopWalk v w) as the parameters.
Thus the first two _ are the graph parameters V and G which are left implicit, u and v' are made
explicit, and the last _ left implicit for the ending vertex of the walk.
-/

/-- The edge associated to the dart. -/
def dartEdge (d : V × V) : Sym2 V :=
  Sym2.mk d

/- The `edges` of a walk is the list of edges it visits in order.
This is defined to be the list of edges underlying `SimpleGraph.LoopWalk.darts`.-/
def edges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) := p.darts.map dartEdge

def connectingEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.connectingDarts.map dartEdge

def selfEdges {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : List (Sym2 V) :=
  p.selfDarts.map dartEdge

def edgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) := (edges p).toFinset

def connectingEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (connectingEdges p).toFinset

def selfEdgeSet {u v: V} [DecidableEq V] (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : Finset (Sym2 V) :=
  (selfEdges p).toFinset


#check countP

/--Edge counting function.-/
def edgeCount {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) (e : Sym2 V) : ℕ := countP (· = e) p.edges

lemma abs_w_i_eq_k {u v : V} (p : G.LoopWalk u v) : ∑(e : edgeSet p),
    edgeCount p e = p.length := by
  sorry
